title: Generalized Error Bounds in the Recovery of Solitary Wave Profiles
abstract: We investigate the robustness of Constantin's explicit reconstruction formula for two-dimensional irrotational solitary water waves. This formula recovers the free-surface profile from the dynamic pressure trace at the bed and depends on both the wave speed and the undisturbed depth. We consider simultaneous perturbations in these three quantities and derive an $L^2$ error estimate for the reconstructed profile. The proof uses the hodograph transform, holomorphic extension arguments, and Paley--Wiener Fourier-decay estimates, yielding stability estimates with sublinear dependence on the perturbation size. We include numerical computations to illustrate the effects of specifically designed perturbations.
summary: This paper rigorously analyzes how accurately the free-surface profile of solitary water waves can be recovered from imperfect measurements of the seabed pressure trace. By employing the hodograph transform and advanced complex analysis, the authors derive stability estimates proving that the reconstruction error grows sublinearly with the size of the measurement perturbations. This work provides a strong mathematical foundation for inverse problems in fluid dynamics.
main_problem: The paper investigates the mathematical robustness and stability of explicit formulas used to reconstruct the free-surface profile of two-dimensional irrotational solitary water waves.
main_result: The authors derive explicit $L^2$ error estimates showing that the reconstruction error depends sublinearly on the size of simultaneous perturbations in wave speed, pressure trace, and undisturbed depth.
method: The analysis employs the hodograph transform to map the free-boundary problem to a fixed domain, followed by advanced complex analysis techniques like holomorphic extension and Paley-Wiener Fourier-decay estimates.
model_or_system: The physical system is two-dimensional irrotational solitary water waves governed by the steady irrotational incompressible Euler equations in a moving frame. The analysis is performed in the hodograph domain.
key_observables: Free-surface profile ($\eta(x)$), dynamic pressure trace ($p(x)$), wave speed ($c$), and undisturbed depth ($d$).
important_parameters: Perturbation sizes ($\epsilon, \delta, \gamma$), wave number $k$, and depth $d$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the no-stagnation condition ($u-c < 0$) and that perturbations are sufficiently small to maintain the validity of the reconstruction formulas.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the Hodograph transform mapping the fluid domain to a fixed strip, and subsequent figures show the dependence of the reconstruction error on perturbations in wave speed, pressure amplitude, and depth.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical problem and initial linear approximations to utilizing the hodograph transform. It then develops the core error analysis using complex methods, culminating in explicit error bounds and numerical validation for various perturbation scenarios.
why_interesting: While the subject is fluid dynamics, the mathematical machinery—relying heavily on complex analysis, Fourier decay estimates, and stability analysis of inverse problems—is highly relevant to the rigorous mathematical treatment of open quantum systems or wave propagation in complex media.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
